---
title: "Foreword to the Revised Edition"
---

# Foreword {.unnumbered}

There is something faintly ridiculous about writing a foreword to one's own work.
Forewords are traditionally the province of distinguished colleagues who lend a
book their credibility by association. But this is a revised edition of a thesis
I wrote twenty-four years ago, and no one alive knows it better — or has more
reason to be suspicious of it — than its original author. So the foreword falls
to me, and I will try to be honest.

In the spring of 2002, I sat at a workstation in NASA Langley Research Center's
Building 1268 in Hampton, Virginia, writing MATLAB code to stitch four disciplinary
analysis programs together into something that could optimise the shape of a
supersonic business jet. The codes were a Fortran-77 structural solver called
ELAPS, a wave-drag program called AWAVE, an empirical engine deck, and the
Breguet range equation. None of them talked to each other naturally. Getting them
to talk — and then building a response surface surrogate over each one, and then
optimising the whole system — took the better part of an academic year.

I remember the computation time. A single pass through the aerodynamics black box,
which ran a linear wave-drag analysis and fitted a 120-point quadratic response
surface in 14 variables, required something like twenty minutes on the shared
cluster. Ten BLISS iterations — the number I eventually used, selected as much for
the approaching thesis deadline as for any principled convergence criterion — took
the better part of a weekend.

I revisited that work in the spring of 2026. The entire BLISS-RS algorithm, all
four black boxes reimplemented in Julia, the response surfaces refitted with
Latin Hypercube Sampling and leave-one-out cross-validation, the interval reduction
logic, the system optimizer — ran to convergence in under four seconds on a laptop
sitting on my kitchen table.

The laptop in question is an Apple M4 MacBook Pro. The year is 2026.

That number — four seconds versus two days — contains most of what motivated this
revision. When I wrote the original thesis, computation was the limiting resource.
Decisions that look questionable in retrospect — fixing the iteration count at 10,
using D-optimal designs augmented with random fallback points when local
optimizations failed, terminating convergence by visual inspection rather than
formal criterion — were made in a context where every additional function
evaluation cost real time. The methodology was shaped by hardware constraints that
no longer exist.

The second motivation was the emergence of tools I simply did not have in 2002.
The Julia language, which compiles to native machine code while reading like
mathematics, made reimplementing the BLISS algorithms a matter of hours rather
than weeks. Gaussian Process surrogates — known in principle in 2002 but
computationally expensive and poorly supported by available libraries — are now
trivial to construct and tune. And I will be candid about something unusual in
academic writing: a significant portion of this revision was written in active
collaboration with an AI coding assistant, which helped me work through
implementation details, identify methodological weaknesses in the original work,
and catch errors I would have missed in a solo review.
That last point deserves a moment's reflection. In 2002, "AI assistance" in
engineering work meant a spell-checker and, if you were lucky, a symbolic algebra
package. What is now possible in an afternoon — a comprehensive independent
review of twenty-four-year-old methodology, identification of specific mathematical
errors (a missing square in Appendix A, a singular error metric, an undocumented
optimizer), and a set of concrete proposed fixes — would have required months of
peer review and a collaborator with deep expertise in every relevant subfield.
The tooling has changed the economics of scholarship, and I think it is worth
saying so plainly.

The third motivation, and perhaps the most honest one, was curiosity.

The BLISS framework was always a conceptual bet: that decomposing a large
multidisciplinary optimization problem into autonomous subsystems, linking them
through surrogate models, and iterating to system-level convergence would be both
computationally tractable and capable of finding designs that a single monolithic
optimizer would also find. In the original work, I could demonstrate tractability —
the algorithm ran and produced improving designs — but I never established optimality.
I never compared the BLISS-RS solution to a monolithic baseline. The thesis was
honest about this, tucking the admission into the conclusions section, but it meant
that the central question was never definitively answered: *Is BLISS-RS a viable
design optimization scheme, or merely a viable surrogate-fitting exercise?*

I want to know. This book is the attempt to find out.

---

I owe debts that a revised edition gives me the chance to acknowledge properly.

The original thesis was completed under the guidance of **Dr. Jaroslaw
Sobieszczanski-Sobieski** at NASA Langley Research Center, whose invention of the
BLISS framework gave me a thesis topic and whose intellectual generosity gave me
room to take it in my own direction. The BLISS-98 architecture described in
Chapter 2 is entirely his work; the response surface reformulation was mine. I hope
this revision does justice to the elegance of the original idea.

I have not yet located Dr. Sobieski to inform him of this work. If you are reading
this and know how to reach him, I would be grateful. If he is no longer with us,
then this foreword serves as my acknowledgment — however belated — of a debt I
should have found more ways to express when the opportunity was easier.

**Prof. Robert Sandusky** of George Washington University co-supervised the thesis
and introduced me to response surface methodology. Lt. Matthew Phillips, U.S. Air
Force, was working concurrent BLISS research and was generous with his time and
insights. Dr. Robert Biedron at NASA Langley helped with the CFD implementation
that became Chapter 8. The improved aerodynamics black box documented there was
never tested in the original work; it is still waiting.

---

A note on the title. The original report was called *A Response Surface Methodology
for Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS)*, which is accurate but not
exactly the kind of title that keeps a reader up at night. The working title
*BLISS-2026* reflects both the year of this revision and the fact that the
framework has been updated — not abandoned, not replaced, but revisited with better
tools and, I hope, more rigorous eyes.

Whether BLISS deserves the name in its fullest sense — whether it is, in the end,
a genuinely good idea — is what the rest of this book tries to determine.

*Troy David Altus*
*Elkton, Maryland*
*May 2026*
